nectar_plants.name: Hibiscus
nectar_plants.notes: Single most important nectar shrub for Bengaluru sunbirds. Red and orange varieties produce the most nectar. Prune after each flowering flush to encourage repeat blooming. Avoid heavy pruning in February–May (breeding season).

nectar_plants.name: Bottlebrush
nectar_plants.notes: Extremely drought-tolerant once established. Red brushes are magnets for sunbirds. Can be trained as a small tree or kept as a hedge. Flowers best with full sun and well-drained laterite soil.

nectar_plants.name: Firecracker plant
nectar_plants.notes: Long tubular red flowers are perfectly adapted to sunbird bills. Cascading habit — excellent on retaining walls, slopes or as a spillover plant. Near drought-proof once established. No serious pest problems.

nectar_plants.name: Ixora
nectar_plants.notes: Prefers slightly acidic soil — common in Bengaluru red laterite. Dense clusters of small tubular flowers. Best as a hedge or mass planting rather than isolated specimens. Red varieties strongly preferred over pink or white.

nectar_plants.name: Orchid tree
nectar_plants.notes: Provides critical nectar during October–January when few other species flower. Deciduous in dry years. Fast growing — can reach 8 m in 5 years. Seed pods should be removed to prevent aggressive self-seeding.

nectar_plants.name: Indian coral tree
nectar_plants.notes: Flowers before the leaves emerge — creating a spectacular red canopy that is extremely visible to birds. Documented in South Indian literature as the most important canopy nectar source for sunbirds in Feb–Apr. Nitrogen-fixing — improves soil. Native to South India.

nectar_plants.name: Jacaranda
nectar_plants.notes: Purple sunbirds show a specific documented preference for Jacaranda blossoms in Bengaluru. Blooms March–May (peak sunbird breeding season). Non-native but strongly established in Bengaluru ecology. Use sparingly — 1–2 per site is sufficient.

nectar_plants.name: Singapore cherry
nectar_plants.notes: Single most reliably documented bird-attracting tree in Bengaluru gardens. Produces tiny sweet fruits year-round that attract flowerpeckers, sunbirds, barbets, and bulbuls simultaneously. Fast growing — reaches 5 m in 3 years. Low maintenance, no serious pests.

nectar_plants.name: Red silk cotton
nectar_plants.notes: Large crimson flowers with abundant nectar — flowers in the December–March dry season when few others bloom. Cup-shaped flowers hold large volumes of nectar. Important roosting and nesting tree. A single specimen can support 10+ bird species. Native to South India.

nectar_plants.name: Firebush
nectar_plants.notes: Tubular orange-red flowers bloom continuously. Tolerates partial shade — useful under canopy trees where Hibiscus will not flower well. Attracts both sunbirds and butterflies. Easy to establish on laterite soils.

canopy_trees.name: Peepal
canopy_trees.notes: Single most important biodiversity tree for South Indian landscapes. Year-round fruiting supports 40+ bird species.

canopy_trees.name: Jamun
canopy_trees.notes: Provides abundant fruit June–August. Dense evergreen canopy supports cavity-nesting species. Native to South India.
